entries.title: Testing authentication mechanisms
entries.body:
# Testing authentication mechanisms

Authentication is the process in which an application makes sure that a user is exactly who they claim to be. If this process is implemented weakly, an attacker gains access to someone else's account, and sometimes to the admin panel too. That is why login forms and the accompanying mechanisms (password recovery, MFA) are one of the first targets during testing.

This section of the Burp documentation describes practical attack scenarios against authentication. All of them, in essence, boil down to one thing: craft a login request and repeatedly substitute different values into it, tracking by what indicators a "successful" login response differs from an "unsuccessful" one.

## Which weaknesses are tested

Burp singles out five typical directions:

* **Username enumeration** - the application reacts differently to an existing and a non-existing login, which lets you collect a list of valid accounts.
* **Guessing usernames (of known people)** - generating logins by patterns (`first.last`, `flast`) for specific employees.
* **Brute-forcing passwords** - guessing the password for a known login by dictionary or mask.
* **Credential stuffing** - checking login:password pairs from third-party leaks (people reuse passwords).
* **Brute-forcing logins** - when neither is known.

## With which Burp tools this is done

* **Intruder** - the main tool. You mark positions (§) in the request, attach dictionaries, and launch the brute force. The attack type (**Sniper**, **Pitchfork**, **Cluster bomb**) is chosen to fit the specific scenario.
* **Repeater** - for manual sending and fine-tuning a single request, to first understand what a failed and a successful login look like.
* **Comparer** - to compare two suspicious responses "side by side" (by words or bytes) and catch a subtle difference.
* **Sequencer** - relates more to sessions, but is useful when you need to check the randomness of tokens issued after login.

## Where to start

```
1. Catch a failed login request in Proxy > HTTP history
   (deliberately wrong login/password).
2. Send it to Repeater, understand the structure of the failure response.
3. Right-click > Send to Intruder, mark the needed positions (§).
4. Choose the attack type and attach dictionaries (Payloads).
5. Start attack > sort the results by Status / Length
   and look for the outlier response.
```

## Practice

The main skill here is learning to see the **indicator of success**. It can be a `302` status (a redirect after login) against a `200`, a different response length (**Length**), a vanished error message, a new cookie in `Set-Cookie`, or a different response time. Before mass brute-forcing always manually fix the "failure" baseline and the "success" baseline through **Repeater** - otherwise in hundreds of rows of Intruder results you will not understand what to look for. And remember about rate limiting and account lockouts: on a live application within bug bounty lower the pace, so as not to take the service down or catch a ban.

entries.title: Username enumeration
entries.body:
# Username enumeration

The idea of the attack: an application often "gives away" whether the entered login exists. If for a non-existing user it answers "Invalid username", and for an existing one with a wrong password - "Invalid password", then from the difference in responses you collect a list of real accounts. This is the foundation for the subsequent password brute force.

The differences can be crude (different error text, different status) and very subtle: one message is missing a period at the end, the length differs by a couple of bytes, the response time is slightly different. The task is to catch any stable difference.

## How it is tested in Burp (Intruder, Sniper)

```
1. In Proxy > HTTP history find the POST of a failed login.
2. Right-click > Send to Intruder.
3. Attack type - Sniper (one position).
4. Clear the auto-markup (Clear §), select the login value
   and click Add § - this is the only position.
5. Payloads: attach a dictionary of names. Professional has
   a built-in Usernames list; in Community paste your own
   (Paste / Load).
6. Start attack.
```

## What to look at in the results

In the results table sort by columns and look for outlier rows:

* **Status code** - a different status on a valid login.
* **Length** - even a difference of a few bytes betrays a different message.
* **Response received** - a noticeably longer response time may mean that for an existing user the server got as far as the password check (or hashing), and for a non-existing one - did not (timing-based enumeration).

To automatically highlight text anomalies, on the **Settings** tab add a **Grep - Match** rule with the error fragment (for example, `Invalid username`). Then a flag column appears in the results: where the error is present and where it is not - that is the valid login.

## Comparing suspicious responses

If the difference is not obvious, select two responses (the baseline and the suspicious one), right-click > **Send to Comparer (response)** and compare in **Comparer** by **Words** or **Bytes** - the differing parts get highlighted.

## Practice

The source of enumeration is not only the login form: the same leaks come from the registration form ("the name is already taken"), password recovery, and MFA steps. First, through **Repeater**, manually enter a deliberately non-existing and (if you know one) an existing user, fix both responses - that way you understand in advance which exact indicator to track in Intruder.

entries.title: Guessing usernames of known users
entries.body:
# Guessing usernames of known users

Often the real people are known (company employees from LinkedIn, article authors, contacts), but the format of their login in the system is not. Organizations generate logins by predictable patterns: `carlos.montoya`, `cmontoya`, `montoyac`, `carlosm`, and so on. Intruder is able to expand a name into such variants by itself, using a special payload type.

## How it is tested in Burp (Username generator)

```
1. In Proxy > HTTP history find the POST of a failed login,
   right-click > Send to Intruder.
2. Attack type - Sniper.
3. Mark the login position (Add §).
4. Payloads > Payload type, choose Username generator.
5. In Payload configuration enter the target's full name
   (for example, Carlos Montoya). You can add several people.
6. In the Maximum payloads per item field set how many login
   variants to generate per entered name.
7. Start attack.
```

**Username generator** will take "Carlos Montoya" and itself produce a set like `carlos.montoya`, `cmontoya`, `mcarlos`, and other common combinations of first name/last name/initials.

## Analyzing the results

As with ordinary enumeration, sort by **Status** and **Length**, look at the response time, and if needed attach a **Grep - Match** on the error text. The goal is the same - to find a login variant that the application reacts to as if it were an existing account.

## Practice

* If the corporate login format is already known (for example, from a leaked email you can see the address `j.smith@company.com`), you do not need the generator: assemble the logins manually into an ordinary list and run it as in username enumeration.
* For real testing combine the approaches: first, through **Username generator**, collect candidates, then confirm the valid ones via enumeration indicators, and only then move on to password guessing.
* Do not crank up **Maximum payloads per item** without need - many variants per name quickly bloat the number of requests and increase the risk of a lockout.

entries.title: Brute-forcing passwords
entries.body:
# Brute-forcing passwords

Scenario: the login is already known (you confirmed it via enumeration, or it is a public account like `administrator`), and it remains to guess the password. In Intruder this is an attack with one position - in the password field - and a fixed valid login.

## How it is tested in Burp (Intruder, Sniper)

```
1. Send the login request to Intruder (Send to Intruder).
2. In the body substitute the known valid login (for example, wiener),
   the login is NOT marked.
3. Attack type - Sniper.
4. Select the password value and click Add § - the only position.
5. Attach a password dictionary (see below).
6. Start attack.
```

## Two ways to set the passwords

### Dictionary (Password list)

On the **Payloads** tab you attach a list of common passwords. Professional has a built-in **Passwords** set; in Community you paste your own (**Paste** / **Load**). A useful trick from the documentation - sort the dictionary by descending likelihood: the most plausible passwords should come first, then the correct one is found faster.

### Full brute force by mask (Brute forcer)

If the password rules are known (for example, exactly a 4-digit PIN), switch **Payload type** to **Brute forcer** and set:

* the character set (**Character set**) - which characters to brute-force;
* the minimum and maximum length.

Burp will go through all combinations. It is important to understand the combinatorics: even a short mask with the whole alphabet yields millions of requests, so a full brute force is justified only for short/narrow formats.

## The indicator of a successful login

Look in the results for the row that stands out:

* **Status** - `302` (a redirect to the account page) instead of `200` with an error form;
* **Length** - a different response size;
* a vanished error message or an appeared session cookie;
* the response time.

For a precise dissection select the suspicious and the baseline responses, right-click > **Send to Comparer (response)** and compare by **Words** / **Bytes**.

## Practice

The main enemy of brute force is the defenses: account lockout after N attempts, CAPTCHA, rate limiting. If you hit an account lockout - switch to **password spraying** (one popular password against many logins, so as not to exceed the attempt limit on a specific account). Regulate the pace via the **Resource pool** (the number of threads and the delay between requests), especially on live targets.

entries.title: Credential stuffing
entries.body:
# Credential stuffing

People reuse passwords. Credential stuffing exploits exactly this: you take a database of login:password pairs from a third-party leak and check it against your application, hoping that someone used the same password. The key difference from brute force: each pair is tried **exactly once**, so a lockout by the number of attempts on a specific account hardly interferes (though you may still need to get around the overall rate limiting).

## How it is tested in Burp (Intruder, Pitchfork)

The pairs must go strictly in sync: the login from row N with its own password from row N. The **Pitchfork** attack type is responsible for this - it moves through the positions "in parallel", taking one element from each list per iteration.

```
1. Login request > Send to Intruder.
2. Attack type - Pitchfork.
3. Mark two positions:
   - select the login > Add §  (position 1)
   - select the password > Add § (position 2)
4. Payloads, Payload set 1: paste the list of LOGINS.
5. Payloads, Payload set 2: paste the list of PASSWORDS
   in the same order (pair to pair).
6. Start attack.
```

Intruder will send one request per pair: (login[1], password[1]), (login[2], password[2]), and so on. It is important that both lists be of the same length and aligned row by row.

## Analyzing the results

As usual when testing login: sort by **Status** (look for `302`/redirect) and **Length**, pay attention to the appearance of a session cookie and the response time. Compare suspicious responses via right-click > **Send to Comparer (response)** by **Words** / **Bytes**.

## Practice

* The source of the pairs is public leak dumps (combolists). Within bug bounty make sure their use does not violate the program's rules and the law.
* Since each account is touched once, the main limitation is the global rate limiting and anti-bot defenses. Regulate the pace via the **Resource pool**.
* If you need to check the same set of passwords against many logins without a pair-to-pair binding - that is already **Cluster bomb** (see brute-forcing logins), not Pitchfork.

entries.title: Brute-forcing login and password at once (brute-forcing logins)
entries.body:
# Brute-forcing login and password at once (brute-forcing logins)

The "heaviest" scenario: neither the login nor the password is known, and you need to check all combinations of one list of logins with all the passwords from another. The **Cluster bomb** attack type is responsible for the full Cartesian brute force - it goes through all combinations of the positions with each other.

## How it is tested in Burp (Intruder, Cluster bomb)

```
1. Login request > Send to Intruder.
2. Attack type - Cluster bomb.
3. Mark two positions:
   - select the login > Add §  (position 1)
   - select the password > Add § (position 2)
4. Payloads, Payload set 1: the list of LOGINS.
5. Payloads, Payload set 2: the list of PASSWORDS.
6. Start attack.
```

Intruder will send a request for **every** combination: each login will be checked with each password. The number of requests is the product of the list lengths (for example, 100 logins x 100 passwords = 10,000 requests), so it is worth keeping the lists compact.

As the documentation advises, sort both dictionaries by descending likelihood - the most plausible logins and passwords up front, so the real pair is found sooner. Ready-made themed dictionaries for such tests are available in the PortSwigger sets.

## Analyzing the results

Look for the outlier row: status **302**/redirect instead of **200**, a different **Length**, an appeared session cookie, a different response time. Dissect suspicious responses via right-click > **Send to Comparer (response)** (**Words** / **Bytes**).

## Cluster bomb vs Pitchfork

This is a frequent confusion:

* **Cluster bomb** - all combinations (each with each). Used when both parts are unknown and you need to go through everything.
* **Pitchfork** - synchronous pairs (row to row). Used for credential stuffing, where each login has its own specific password.

## Practice

Because of the explosive growth in the number of requests, keep the list of logins short (for example, ones confirmed via enumeration), and focus the brute force on the passwords. Regulate the pace and parallelism via the **Resource pool**; on live targets add a delay, so as not to hit rate limiting and not take the login form down.

entries.title: Testing session management mechanisms
entries.body:
# Testing session management mechanisms

HTTP does not keep state, so, to avoid forcing the user to log in on every click, the server issues a **session token** (usually a cookie) and by it "recognizes" the user between requests. If this mechanic is implemented weakly, an attacker intercepts or forges the token and works under someone else's identity (session hijacking) - without a login and password.

This section describes how Burp helps check the robustness of session management.

## Which weaknesses are tested

* **Weak token generation** - the tokens are predictable (based on time, a counter, a weak PRNG), they can be computed or guessed.
* **Timeout problems** - the session lives too long or does not end on logout, widening the window for hijacking.
* **CSRF** - the application accepts an action "by cookie", without verifying that the request was initiated by the user themselves.
* **JWT vulnerabilities** - weak or absent signature verification, `alg: none`, a weak secret.
* **Hijacking and impersonation** - the general outcome of any of the listed problems.

## Burp tools

* **Sequencer** - statistically evaluates the randomness of a set of tokens (the entropy), revealing predictability.
* **Repeater** - manual checking: resending a request with an old/foreign token, checking the timeout and logout.
* **Proxy** - interception of tokens and traffic; **Match and replace** rules for mass substitution.
* **Intruder** - enumeration/brute force of tokens and, for example, measuring the timeout via a series of requests with a growing delay.
* **Engagement tools > Generate CSRF PoC** - generation of an HTML exploit to check CSRF.

## Subsections

* analysis of session token generation (Sequencer);
* session timeout;
* generation of a CSRF proof-of-concept;
* working with JWT;
* maintaining an authenticated session during automated testing.

## Practice

It is convenient to start with the most "juicy" thing - the session cookie right after login: send the response with `Set-Cookie` to **Sequencer** and evaluate the entropy. In parallel keep the cookie attributes in mind (`HttpOnly`, `Secure`, `SameSite`) - their absence amplifies the risk of token theft via XSS or CSRF. Next, check what happens to the old token after logout and on expiry (via **Repeater**).

entries.title: Analyzing session token generation
entries.body:
# Analyzing session token generation

A robust session identifier must be unpredictable. If tokens are generated predictably (from time, an incremental counter, a weak PRNG), an attacker computes or guesses someone else's token and hijacks an active session without a password. This is checked with the **Sequencer** tool, which collects many tokens and statistically evaluates their randomness in bits of entropy.

## How it is tested in Burp (Sequencer)

```
1. Log in to the application via Burp's browser, to obtain
   a session cookie.
2. Open Proxy > HTTP history and find the response that issues the token
   (Set-Cookie with the session identifier).
3. Right-click that entry > Send to Sequencer.
4. On the Sequencer tab, in the Token location within response panel,
   specify WHERE the token sits - choose the right cookie.
5. Click Start live capture - Burp will start repeating
   the request many times and accumulating tokens.
6. Once enough have accumulated (at least ~100, reliably - thousands),
   click Analyze now.
```

## How to read the result

* The **Summary** tab gives the overall randomness assessment and the effective entropy in **bits**.
* **High** entropy (tens to hundreds of bits) - the tokens are robust, brute force is impossible.
* **Low** entropy (single bits or sharply less than the token length) - an alarming signal: the tokens have structure, they are potentially predictable.
* The **Character-level** and **Bit-level** tabs show at which positions/bits the randomness is "lost".

## Important nuances

* **Sample size.** On a hundred tokens you can only "feel things out"; make a conclusion about weakness on thousands. Some detailed tests on a small sample are disabled as unreliable.
* **Encoding.** If the token looks like Base64, enable **Base64-decode before analyzing** in the Sequencer settings, otherwise the entropy will be distorted by the limited base64 alphabet.
* **Collection pace.** Live capture drives many requests - on a live target reduce the number of threads and add a delay, so as not to catch a lockout.

## Practice

Sequencer gives a strong statistical argument, but it is better to get the final confirmation by understanding the generation mechanism. If the entropy is low - collect several tokens in a row and look for a pattern by eye: it may turn out to be hex of time, an increment of a counter, or base64 of a predictable string. A proven predictability of the session token = an authentication bypass, a serious finding for bug bounty.

entries.title: Session timeout
entries.body:
# Session timeout

A session must automatically expire after a period of inactivity. If the timeout is too long (or there is none at all), a stolen or forgotten token stays valid for a long time - the window for an attack stretches out. The task of the test is to measure after exactly how much idle time the token stops working.

This is measured like so: the same authorized request is sent over and over with an ever-larger delay, and you watch at which interval the server stops recognizing the session (for example, starts redirecting to login).

## How it is tested in Burp (Intruder, Null payloads)

```
1. Log in to Burp's browser.
2. In Proxy > HTTP history take any authorized request
   (for example, to /my-account) and send it to Intruder.
3. There is no need to mark positions - what matters to us is repeating one request.
4. Payloads > Payload type = Null payloads.
   Choose Continue indefinitely (send endlessly).
5. Create a separate Resource pool and enable in it
   Delay between requests with a GROWING delay
   (incremental, +N ms on each next request).
6. Start attack - Burp will repeat the request with an ever-larger pause.
```

## How to determine the moment of expiry

* Add a **Time of day** column to the results table (via the displayed columns setting) and sort by time.
* Find the first response that says the session is already invalid - for example, a redirect (**302**) to the login page or a different status/length.
* The difference between this request and the previous successful one gives an estimate of the timeout.

## Practice

* Separately check the behavior on **logout**: after exiting, take the old token and resend an authorized request in **Repeater**. If it still works - the server did not invalidate the session on exit (a frequent and important finding).
* The growing delay is needed so as not to send thousands of requests in vain: you are, in a sense, "probing" ever-longer idle intervals.
* Account for the difference between an absolute timeout (a hard limit on the session's lifetime) and an idle timeout (by inactivity) - repeated requests reset exactly the idle timer, so the method measures precisely the pauses without activity.

entries.title: Generating a CSRF proof-of-concept
entries.body:
# Generating a CSRF proof-of-concept

CSRF (cross-site request forgery) is when an application performs an action "by the user's cookie", without verifying that the request was initiated by the user themselves. Then a malicious page on another site can, on behalf of a logged-in victim, send a request (for example, change the email). To prove the vulnerability you need an HTML page that reproduces this request. Burp assembles it automatically.

## How to generate it in Burp (Generate CSRF PoC)

```
1. Find a suspicious request that performs a meaningful action
   (changing email/password, a transfer, etc.) - in HTTP history,
   Repeater, or via Scanner.
2. Right-click the request > Engagement tools > Generate CSRF PoC.
3. A window opens: at the top the original request, at the bottom the generated
   HTML exploit.
```

## Options in the window

* **CSRF technique** - by what means to form the request in the browser. By default **Auto**: Burp itself chooses a suitable technique (an HTML form and/or JavaScript) capable of reproducing the needed request. Worth changing only if the auto-selection did not cope with a non-standard request.
* **Include auto-submit script** - add to the HTML a script that itself submits the request when the page loads (the victim does not need to click anything). Ticking the checkbox is a frequent choice for a realistic PoC.
* **Regenerate** - reassemble the HTML after edits (for example, if you changed the field values or the options).
* **Copy HTML** - copy the ready exploit.
* **Test in browser** - open the PoC in Burp's browser, to check whether it will work.
* **Options** - access to the settings described above.

## The full check cycle

```
1. In the generated HTML, if needed, fix the field values
   (for example, substitute the attacker's email) and click Regenerate.
2. Copy the HTML (Copy HTML) to the exploit server or save it
   locally as .html.
3. Open the page in a browser that ALREADY has an active session
   on the vulnerable application.
4. Look in Proxy > HTTP history / in the response whether the
   action was performed (for example, whether the email really changed).
5. Confirm on a different account that CSRF protection is absent.
```

## Practice

If the PoC does not work - almost always a defense is to blame: an anti-CSRF token in the request, a check of the `Origin`/`Referer` headers, or a cookie with `SameSite`. Check via **Repeater** whether the request goes through at all without an anti-CSRF token and from a third-party context. For bug bounty the report includes exactly this HTML plus the reproduction steps.

entries.title: Working with JWT
entries.body:
# Working with JWT

A JWT (JSON Web Token) is a signed JSON structure of three parts (header.payload.signature), which applications often use for authentication and access control. The danger is that if an application verifies the signature incorrectly (or does not verify it at all), an attacker edits the payload - for example, changes `"role":"user"` to `"admin"` or substitutes someone else's `sub` - and escalates privileges or impersonates another person.

## What to view and edit with in Burp

* **Inspector** - decodes a JWT inline: select the token in the request, and the Inspector shows the contents of the header and payload in readable form (without the signature there is no point editing the token, but reading the claims is useful right away).
* **JWT Editor** - an extension from the **BApp Store** that gives real editing and re-signing capabilities. After installation a **JSON Web Token** tab appears in the message editor, and at the top level - a **JWT Editor Keys** tab for managing keys.

## How it is tested in Burp (JWT Editor)

```
1. Install JWT Editor from the BApp Store.
2. Send the request with the token to Repeater.
3. Open the JSON Web Token tab - the header and payload are shown
   as editable JSON fields.
4. Change the needed claims (for example, the role or the identifier).
5. Click Sign and choose a key for re-signing, then send
   the request and look at the server's reaction.
```

Keys are added on the **JWT Editor Keys** tab: you can generate new symmetric/asymmetric keys or import existing ones (the JWK/PEM formats); keys are saved between sessions.

## Typical attacks that are checked this way

* **No signature verification** - we change the payload, leave any signature: if the server accepted it - it does not verify the signature.
* **`alg: none`** - we set the algorithm to "none" and remove the signature: if accepted - the classic bypass.
* **Weak secret (HS256)** - the secret is guessed offline by dictionary (for example, hashcat); having found it, we re-sign the modified token in **JWT Editor** with a valid signature.
* **Key substitution / kid injections** - manipulations with the `jku`, `kid` headers, and the like.

Many of these scenarios JWT Editor is able to apply semi-automatically.

## Practice

Start by reading the payload via **Inspector** - sometimes the role/rights that are tempting to play with are immediately visible there. Next move on to **Repeater** + **JWT Editor**: you change one claim, re-sign (or deliberately break the signature) and look at whether the server swallows it. Check each change with a separate request, to understand which exact weakness fired.

entries.title: Maintaining an authenticated session during testing
entries.body:
# Maintaining an authenticated session during testing

During automated testing (Scanner, Intruder) the application can "kick" you out of the session at any moment - for example, after suspicious input or by timeout. After that all requests go as unauthenticated, and the test results are meaningless. The solution is **session handling rules**: a rule itself notices that the session has gone stale and, via a **macro**, logs in again, refreshing the cookies.

## What this is made of

* **Session handling rule** - a rule: to which tools and URLs it applies and what to do on an invalid session.
* **Macro** - a recorded sequence of login requests that the rule plays back.
* **Cookie jar** - Burp's shared cookie storage, where the macro puts the fresh token.

## Configuration in three steps

### 1. Understand what an "invalid session" looks like

```
1. Log in to Burp's browser, open a protected page
   (for example, My Account).
2. Log out and try to open the same page again.
3. In Proxy > HTTP history look at the refusal indicator
   (for example, a 302 redirect to /login).
```
This indicator will be needed by the rule to detect the session drop.

### 2. Create a rule

```
1. Settings > Sessions > Session handling rules > Add.
2. The Scope tab: mark the tools (Scanner, Intruder,
   Repeater) and specify the application's URL.
3. The Details tab: set the rule's name.
4. Rule actions > Add > Check session is valid. In the editor:
   - Inspect response location: where to look (URL / cookies / body);
   - Look for expression: the invalid-session indicator (for example, login);
   - Match type: Literal string or regex;
   - Match indicates: Invalid session (found the indicator = the session dropped).
5. In the behavior block choose:
   If session is invalid, perform the action below > Run a macro.
```

### 3. Record the login macro

```
1. The Add button opens the Macro recorder.
2. Select the login requests in order
   (usually a GET of the login page, then a POST with the credentials).
3. OK - the macro is saved.
```

## Verification

Log out of the application, take any "logged-in" request from HTTP history, send it to **Repeater**, and run it. If the rule works, Burp itself will notice the invalid session, play the macro, refresh the cookies, and return a response as for an authorized user.

## Practice

* This is the basis for stable scanning: without a working session handling rule **Scanner** will stubbornly test the login page instead of the protected functionality.
* Choose a reliable and unambiguous invalidity indicator (a unique text or a redirect), otherwise the rule will fire falsely or, on the contrary, miss the drop.
* For access testing (see the Access controls section) the same mechanism is used differently - not for re-login, but to forcibly substitute a specific user's cookie via a rule action.

entries.title: Testing access controls
entries.body:
# Testing access controls

Access control determines who is entitled to do or view what in an application. If it is implemented incorrectly, an ordinary user reaches admin functions or someone else's data. This is one of the most frequent and at the same time high-rank vulnerability categories in bug bounty, because the business logic of authorization is easy to under-test.

## Which weaknesses are tested

* **IDOR (Insecure Direct Object References)** - a request features a direct reference to an object (an id, a file name), and, by substituting it, you can open someone else's resource.
* **Vertical privilege escalation** - a low-privileged user gains the functions of a more privileged one (an admin).
* **Horizontal access controls** - a user reaches the data of another user with the same rights.
* **Parameter-based access control** - the rights are determined by a request parameter (`role=user`, `isAdmin=false`) that can be forged.

## Burp tools

* **Repeater** - a targeted check of a single endpoint: substitute an id/parameter or a foreign cookie and look at the response.
* **Intruder** - brute force of identifiers (for example, numeric ids) to find IDOR.
* **Proxy > Match and replace** - mass substitution of a token/parameter in all requests during a repeated walk of the application.
* **Target > Site map + Compare site maps** - compare site maps captured from different users, to see in bulk where access is not restricted.
* **Session handling rules** - automatically substitute the needed user's cookie when comparing maps.

## Subsections

* testing IDOR;
* parameter-based access control;
* vertical privilege escalation;
* horizontal access control;
* IP address spoofing via Match and replace.

## The general principle of the test

Almost all access checks are built on one idea: perform an action/open a resource **in a context in which it is forbidden**, and make sure the application really refuses. In practice this means having two accounts (a low- and a high-privileged one or two equal ones) and checking that the request of one, performed as the other (via substitution of a cookie/parameter/id), gets a refusal (for example, **401**/**403** or a redirect), rather than being performed.

## Practice

For mass checks the **Autorize** extension from the BApp Store helps a lot: it automatically resends your requests as a low-privileged user and highlights where access is mistakenly allowed. This is a fast alternative to manually configuring session handling rules and comparing site maps.

entries.title: Privilege escalation (vertical access control)
entries.body:
# Privilege escalation (vertical access control)

Vertical privilege escalation is when a low-privileged user reaches functions intended for a more privileged one (for example, an ordinary user opens the admin panel). The essence of the test: take a request available to the admin and perform it as an ordinary user - if the application does not refuse, access control is broken.

## Way 1. Targeted via Repeater

```
1. Log in as the admin in Burp's browser, open the protected
   function (the admin panel). In HTTP history find this request.
2. Log in as an ordinary user (in another window/after
   logout) and copy their session cookie from a fresh request
   in HTTP history.
3. Send the admin request to Repeater.
4. Substitute in it the cookie for the low-privileged
   user's cookie and run the request.
5. Look at the response: if an access error (401/403) came back -
   the control works; if the admin function came - a vulnerability.
```

## Way 2. Automatically across the whole application (Compare site maps)

```
1. Walk the application as the admin, populating Target > Site map
   (be sure to enter the admin sections).
2. Log out, log in as an ordinary user, copy their
   session cookie.
3. Settings > Sessions > Session handling rules > Add:
   - Scope: only the Target tool and your application's URL;
   - Details > rule action: Set a specific cookie (or Set value),
     substitute the session cookie name and the ordinary user's value.
4. Target > Site map, right-click the host > Compare site maps.
5. Choose Request map 1 again in a different session context -
   Burp will recapture the map, but now with the ordinary user's
   cookie (the rule will substitute it).
6. Compare the maps: the highlighted differences point to endpoints
   where low-privileged access behaves "like an admin".
```

## What counts as a finding

* Access to an admin function as an ordinary user (the response matches the admin one instead of 401/403).
* Special attention - to "hidden" admin URLs: if the defense rests only on the fact that "nobody knows" the link (security by obscurity), that is already a vulnerability.

## Practice

* Way 1 is fast for one or two suspicious endpoints; way 2 is needed to cover the whole application at once.
* An alternative to manual configuration is the **Autorize** extension: it itself duplicates each of your admin requests as a low-privileged user and marks the result (Bypassed / Enforced).
* Do not forget about the vector "an admin function called by the wrong method/the wrong Content-Type" - sometimes the defense hangs only on a specific request method.

entries.title: Horizontal access control
entries.body:
# Horizontal access control

A horizontal access vulnerability is when a user reaches the data of **another user with the same rights** (for example, reads someone else's order or profile). For the test you need two accounts of the same level: you try to seize the resource/request of the first user from under the second.

## Way 1. Targeted via Repeater

```
1. Log in as the first user, open their data
   (for example, your own profile/order). Find the request in HTTP history.
2. Log in as a second user of the same level and copy
   their session cookie.
3. Send the first user's request to Repeater.
4. Substitute the cookie for the second user's cookie and run it.
5. If the response shows the FIRST user's data - the application
   does not verify resource ownership, this is a vulnerability.
```

The same applies not only to the cookie: if the resource is addressed by a parameter (`?id=...`, a file name), try from under the second user to reference the first user's identifier - this overlaps with IDOR.

## Way 2. Across the whole application (Compare site maps)

```
1. Walk the application as the first user, populating
   Target > Site map.
2. Copy the second user's session cookie.
3. Settings > Sessions > Session handling rules > Add:
   - Scope: only Target and the application's URL;
   - rule action: substitute the session cookie with the value
     of the SECOND user.
4. Target > Site map, right-click the host > Compare site maps.
5. Choose Use current site map and Request map 1 again in a
   different session context - Burp will recapture the map with the
   second user's cookie.
6. Compare the maps. Here the logic is the reverse of vertical:
   IDENTICAL responses are suspicious - it means the second user
   sees the same as the first (someone else's data).
```

## Practice

* A subtlety of interpretation: in a horizontal test the dangerous thing is **matches** (the second sees the first's data), whereas in a vertical one - differences (the low-privileged one got an admin response).
* The most frequent places are endpoints of the kind "my profile / my order / my document", where the object id rides in the URL or the body. Change the identifier to one belonging to another account.
* For mass checking **Autorize** is more convenient: you set the second user's cookie in it, walk the application as the first - the extension immediately shows where foreign access is not blocked.

entries.title: Testing for IDOR
entries.body:
# Testing for IDOR

IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference) is a particular, but very common, case of broken access control: an application accepts a direct reference to an object (a numeric id, a file name, a GUID) and serves the resource, without verifying that it is yours. By substituting the identifier, an attacker opens someone else's data.

## Where to look

First we find where there are identifiers in requests at all:

* URL parameters (`?id=123`, `?user=wiener`);
* fields in the request body;
* cookie values;
* API paths (`/api/orders/123`).

Direct references like a username or a sequential numeric id in the URL are a strong indicator of a potential IDOR.

## Way 1. Manual check in Repeater

```
1. Send the suspicious request to Repeater.
2. Substitute the identifier value (for example, id=123 -> id=124
   or your username -> someone else's).
3. Look at the response: did someone else's data come back.
```

This is a fast way to confirm the idea on one or two values.

## Way 2. Brute force of identifiers in Intruder

```
1. Request from HTTP history > Send to Intruder.
2. Attack type - Sniper.
3. Select the identifier value > Add § (one position).
4. Payloads:
   - for numeric ids: Payload type = Numbers, set a range
     (for example, from 1 to 100) and a step;
   - for textual ids/logins: the Usernames list (Pro) or your own set.
5. Start attack.
```

## Analyzing the results

Sort the results and look for successful accesses to someone else's resources:

* **Status** - `200 OK` where a refusal was expected;
* **Length** - a different response size betrays that real data came back, not a stub;
* the response content (someone else's names, amounts, documents);
* the response time.

To dissect suspicious responses use right-click > **Send to Comparer (response)**.

## Practice

* If the identifiers are **GUIDs/UUIDs**, a direct brute force is useless (the space is enormous). Then look for where the application itself "leaks" foreign GUIDs - in lists, logs, responses of other endpoints, in `Location` headers - and substitute the ones you already know.
* Check not only reading, but also modification/deletion by someone else's id (a write IDOR is more dangerous).
* IDOR is closely related to horizontal access control: the same endpoint is worth checking both with id substitution and with the substitution of another user's cookie.

entries.title: Parameter-based access control
entries.body:
# Parameter-based access control

Sometimes an application determines rights right from a value in the request: `role=user`, `isAdmin=false`, `access=read`, a hidden form field, or a cookie with an access level. If the server trusts this parameter and does not re-verify the rights on the backend, it is enough to forge the value (`false` -> `true`, `user` -> `admin`) to widen access.

## Where to look

First map the visible surface of the application (walk through it), then via a search over Burp's traffic find suspicious values. The documentation advises looking for strings like `false` and `read` - these are often markers of access parameters. Look at query parameters, the request body, hidden form fields, and cookies.

## How it is tested in Burp

### Targeted - in Repeater

```
1. Send the request with the suspicious parameter to Repeater.
2. Change the value (isAdmin=false -> true, role=user -> admin).
3. Run it and look at whether extended access appeared.
```

### Mass - via Match and replace

To check the parameter across the whole application at once, it is more convenient to substitute it automatically in all requests:

```
1. Proxy > Match and replace (or Settings > Tools > Proxy).
2. Under HTTP match and replace rules click Add.
3. Choose the type (Type) corresponding to the parameter's location:
   Request header / Request body / Request param value.
4. In the Match field specify the original value (for example, false),
   in the Replace field - the needed one (true). You can create several rules
   for different parameters.
5. Enable the rule and walk the application again -
   Burp will substitute the parameter in each request on the fly.
6. Compare whether new functions/data appeared.
```

## What counts as a finding

Any widening of access after substitution: an admin button appeared, a hidden section opened, data that should not exist came back. Even if the server held up against substitution in the action itself, the changed parameter sometimes reveals additional surface on the client (hidden links, an admin interface) - that is also a lead for further testing.

## Practice

* Check access parameters in all places: URL, body, hidden fields, cookies - sometimes the defense is present in one place and forgotten in another.
* **Match and replace** is good precisely for the wholesale re-marking of the application "as if I am an admin"; for targeted confirmation **Repeater** is faster.
* Remember that a client-side flag (`isAdmin` in the response/JS) does not grant rights by itself - what matters is whether the server actually performs the action; always confirm a finding with a real privileged action.

entries.title: IP address spoofing via Match and replace in Proxy
entries.body:
# IP address spoofing via Match and replace in Proxy

Some applications determine "local" access by the client's IP address, trusting headers like `X-Forwarded-For` or `X-Custom-IP-Authorization`. For example, the admin panel is "open only from 127.0.0.1". If the server reads the IP from such a header, it can be forged by adding the header to each of your requests. It is most convenient to do this automatically via a **Match and replace** rule in Proxy.

## How to set up the rule

```
1. Proxy > Match and replace (the HTTP match and replace rules section).
2. Click Add - the Add match/replace rule dialog opens.
3. For Type choose Request header.
4. Leave the Match field EMPTY - this way the header will be ADDED,
   not replace an existing one.
5. In the Replace field enter the whole header, for example:
   X-Custom-IP-Authorization: 127.0.0.1
   (or X-Forwarded-For: 127.0.0.1).
6. Click Test and check in the Auto-modified request block
   that the header was really added.
7. OK - the rule is saved and enabled.
```

After this Burp will automatically add the header to each request going through Proxy (during an ordinary walk of the application in Burp's browser), without manual intervention.

## How to check that it worked

* Open the target page (for example, the admin section) via Burp's browser.
* Make sure in **Proxy > HTTP history** or in **Logger** that your header is present in the outgoing request (you can switch the display between original and modified).
* If access opened - the application mistakenly trusts the IP from the header.

## Why leave Match empty

This is the key point: an empty **Match** field means "add a new header". If you wanted to replace an already existing header, in **Match** you would specify its current value/name. For IP spoofing the add mode is usually needed precisely because such headers are not present in the browser's original request.

## Practice

* Go through different header names: `X-Forwarded-For`, `X-Real-IP`, `X-Originating-IP`, `X-Custom-IP-Authorization`, `X-Client-IP`, and so on - applications read different ones.
* Match and replace is convenient for a wholesale walk "as a trusted IP"; for a targeted check of a single request the same header can simply be added by hand in **Repeater**.
* Do not forget to disable the rule after the test, otherwise it will keep affecting all subsequent traffic and distort other checks.
